title: iOS26 SwiftUI for Web Developers
text:
Important



This is *not a legitimate Oreilly publishing, I hope it is one day.



Preface
This is a book aimed at others on a similar journey of specialisation in Swift - from the world of React and web development.
This book assumes the reader has some programming experience and does not explain basics
Writing this book as I ask AI to explain concepts to me, and making a shareable resource in the process
The dogs on the cover are Annie and Anubis they are adopted Greyhounds, recommend this breed to any software engineer wanting dogs - they are couch potatoes and will not bother you while you code.
This is dedicated to my wonderful partner Alex; who showed me the world of software engineering and gave me the power to build anything I could dream of. Thankyou for believing in me, and pushing me to learn to code. You were right, it rocks.

title: Anatomy of a Basic App
text:
The Folder Structure
Every iOS project natively uses Xcode as the development environment. The folder structure relies on specific files existing in order for the application to compile correctly.
High-Level Xcode Structure:
crumbs: 2  Anatomy of a Basic App

title: Anatomy of a Basic App
text:
App.swift — The root entry point of the app.
ContentView.swift — The first screen the user actually sees.
Assets.xcassets — Where you store images, custom colors, and app icons.
Info.plist — Configuration file containing hardware permissions and app rules.







NoteWhat does this look like in React?





React projects are more flexible, but generally map similarly:

index.js (Root Entry)
App.js (Main Router/Screen)
public/ or src/assets/ (Images/Icons)
package.json (Configuration)
crumbs: 2  Anatomy of a Basic App

title: Objects & Data Structures
text: If numbers are objects, what happens if you build a custom object and try to add them together with +?

title: Objects & Data Structures
text:
Structs (Value Types)
The struct is the backbone of Swift and the default choice for data modeling (and all SwiftUI views).






TipJS / React Perspective



React requires state to be immutable (never mutating variables directly; always using setState). Swift enforces immutability automatically with structs. When you pass a struct, Swift creates an entirely new, disconnected physical copy in memory.


Key Traits:

Behavior: Copied on assignment.
Safety: Modifying a copy does not alter the original.
Inheritance: None. (Use Protocols instead).

struct User {
    var name: String
    var role: String
}

var admin = User(name: "Alice", role: "Admin")
var backupAdmin = admin // Creates a distinct, physical copy in memory

backupAdmin.name = "Bob"
// admin.name safely remains "Alice"

title: Objects & Data Structures
text:
Classes (Reference Types)
Classes are used less frequently in modern Swift than in older object-oriented languages. You use them primarily for data that must be shared across different screens simultaneously (like ViewModels).






TipJS / React Perspective



A Swift class behaves exactly like a standard JavaScript Object. If you pass it to a new variable, you are just passing a pointer. Changing one changes them all.


Key Traits:

Behavior: Shared reference in memory.
Safety: Mutable across your entire app (requires careful management).
Inheritance: Can inherit from parent classes.

class DatabaseConnection {
    var isConnected: Bool = false
}

let db1 = DatabaseConnection()
let db2 = db1 // Points to the exact same memory address

db2.isConnected = true
// db1.isConnected is now also true

title: Objects & Data Structures
text:
Enums (Enumerations)
Enums in Swift are first-class, memory-safe structures used to represent a group of related, mutually exclusive states.






TipJS / React Perspective



In TypeScript, you enforce state using Union Strings (type Status = "success" | "error" | "loading"). Swift enums are the native, highly-optimized equivalent, but they can also carry variables directly inside them.


Key Traits:

Behavior: Value type.
Superpower: Can attach specific variables directly to specific cases (“Associated Values”).
Usage: Replaces boolean flags (isLoading, hasError) and magic strings.

enum LoadingState {
    case idle
    case loading
    case success([String]) // Data is attached safely inside the case
    case error(Error)
}

let currentState = LoadingState.success(["Item 1", "Item 2"])

title: Objects & Data Structures
Variables
text:
a Variable is an object reference
let is an immutable reference - its value is assigned once and cannot be changed
var is a mutable reference - its value can be changed




let-var.png



Variables have a type which is established when the variable is declared and cannot be changed later.

type annotation is optional in Swift
type inference is the process of deducing the type of a variable from its initial value


var name = "Charlotte"         // inferred as String
name = 26                     // Error: Cannot assign value of type 'Int' to type 'String'

variables start with small letter
types start with uppercase letter
A variable declared at the top level of a file is a global variable


Object Members
class fly {
    let name = "buzz"
    func sayName() {
        print(name)
    }
}

name here is a property of the object

a variable declared at the top level of an object declaration

sayName here is a method of the object

a function declared at the top level of an object declaration


If declared at the top level of an object declaration - it’s an object member. Members of an object define messages that can be sent to the object.

title: Functions
Scope
text:
Levels of scope:

a module is a scope
a function is a scope
curly braces are a scope

title: Global Namespace
text:
Namespaces
In JavaScript, there is always a global object — window in the browser, global in Node.js. Any variable you declare with var at the top level automatically becomes a property on that object, and you can inspect or mutate the global scope at runtime.
Swift has no such object. There is no window, no global, nothing to inspect. Instead, the global namespace in Swift is your module — the compiled unit of code that makes up your app or framework. It is a flat, statically-typed scope that the compiler fully resolves before your app ever runs.
A namespace is a named region of code. In Swift, any type that contains nested types creates its own namespace. Nested names cannot be accessed without the dot operator .:

title: Global Namespace
text:
class Dog {             // Dog is a namespace (because it contains a nested class)
    class Annie {}      // Annie is a nested type
}
   
Dog.Annie               // ✅ Correct — must go through Dog
Annie                   // ❌ Compile error — Annie does not exist at the top level






NoteReact Parallel





This is similar to named exports. If Annie is a named export inside a Dog module, you cannot use Annie directly without importing or destructuring it from Dog first.
import { Annie } from './Dog'  // explicit — similar to Dog.Annie

title: Global Namespace
Global Namespace
text:
What’s in the Global Namespace?
Anything declared at the top level of a Swift file — meaning outside of any struct, class, enum, or func — lives in the module’s global namespace and is accessible from every other file in the same target without any import:







What you can declare globally
Example




Types (struct, class, enum, actor, protocol)
struct User { }


Global variables and constants
let appVersion = "1.0"


Top-level functions
func greet() { }


Extensions
extension String { }


Operators
func +(lhs: Point, rhs: Point) -> Point { }


typealias
typealias UserID = String



By default, these are internal — visible to every file in your module, but not visible to other modules (other frameworks or apps that might import yours). You can widen this to public if you are building a library.






NoteReact Parallel — No Direct Equivalent





JavaScript modules require explicit exports. Swift’s internal is more like writing a variable in a shared module store — everything in the same app can see it without you doing anything extra.
// JS — you must export explicitly
export const appVersion = "1.0"

// Swift — no export needed within the same module
let appVersion = "1.0"  // every file in the app can use this

title: Global Namespace
section: How to Write to the Global Namespace
